edges.description: GBA1 mutations reduce or abolish glucocerebrosidase enzyme activity.
Loss of glucocerebrosidase activity impairs lysosomal degradation of glucosylceramide.
Very low residual GCase activity in severe genotypes permits neuronal glycosphingolipid storage.
intermediate_mechanisms: low residual GCase activity in neuronopathic genotypes

edges.description: Reduced GCase activity can impair autophagy and alpha-synuclein turnover, linking GBA1 mutations to Parkinson disease risk.
Undegraded glucosylceramide accumulates primarily in macrophages.
GCase deficiency favors alternative conversion of glucosylceramide to glucosylsphingosine.
Lipid-laden macrophages become Gaucher cells.
Gaucher cells infiltrate spleen and liver, causing reticuloendothelial organ enlargement.
Gaucher cell infiltration of marrow disrupts hematopoietic space and contributes to cytopenias.
edges.target: Bone Marrow Infiltration and Hematopoietic Suppression

edges.description: Marrow Gaucher cells and lipid-driven vascular compression initiate skeletal involvement.
edges.target: Gaucher Skeletal Remodeling and Bone Infarction

edges.description: Gaucher and activated monocyte/macrophage populations release cytokines, chemokines, chitotriosidase, and CCL18.
Liver infiltration by Gaucher cells enlarges the liver.
Spleen infiltration by Gaucher cells enlarges the spleen.
Marrow infiltration and splenic involvement reduce circulating platelet counts.
edges.source: Bone Marrow Infiltration and Hematopoietic Suppression

edges.description: Marrow infiltration contributes to reduced red-cell output and anemia.
edges.source: Bone Marrow Infiltration and Hematopoietic Suppression

edges.description: Cytopenias and systemic Gaucher disease burden can manifest as fatigue.
edges.source: Bone Marrow Infiltration and Hematopoietic Suppression

edges.description: Activated Gaucher macrophages release chitotriosidase as a disease-burden biomarker.
Inflammatory mediators stimulate bone resorption and contribute to skeletal disease.
edges.target: Gaucher Skeletal Remodeling and Bone Infarction

edges.description: Glucosylsphingosine accumulation in brain contributes to neuronal dysfunction and death.
Sphingolipid imbalance, including sphingosine generated from glucosylsphingosine metabolism, contributes to skeletal toxicity.
edges.target: Gaucher Skeletal Remodeling and Bone Infarction

edges.description: Skeletal infiltration, bone crises, and infarction cause recurrent bone pain.
edges.source: Gaucher Skeletal Remodeling and Bone Infarction

edges.description: Dysregulated Gaucher skeletal remodeling lowers bone mass and produces osteopenia.
edges.source: Gaucher Skeletal Remodeling and Bone Infarction

edges.description: Low bone mass and disordered remodeling increase pathological fracture risk.
edges.source: Gaucher Skeletal Remodeling and Bone Infarction

edges.description: Abnormal skeletal remodeling produces the characteristic distal femur deformity.
edges.source: Gaucher Skeletal Remodeling and Bone Infarction

edges.description: Neuronal lipid storage and injury are accompanied by microgliosis and astrogliosis.
Neuronal lipid toxicity causes neuronal dysfunction and death, producing oculomotor, bulbar, pyramidal, seizure, and developmental phenotypes.
Microgliosis and astrogliosis accompany neuronal loss and amplify neuronopathic disease.
Oculomotor pathway dysfunction causes impaired voluntary saccades in acute neuronopathic disease.
Chronic neuronopathic disease commonly affects horizontal supranuclear gaze.
Progressive neuronal dysfunction produces seizures and myoclonic epilepsy.
Chronic neuronopathic disease may progress to myoclonus epilepsy.
Bulbar dysfunction in acute neuronopathic disease causes swallowing impairment.
Pyramidal tract involvement produces progressive spasticity and rigidity.
Early neuronopathic injury alters psychomotor development.
Oculomotor pathway involvement may present as bilateral fixed strabismus.
Severe neuronopathic and systemic disease in infancy contributes to growth failure and cachexia.
Impaired alpha-synuclein proteostasis increases Parkinson disease risk and can manifest clinically as parkinsonism.

evidence.reference_title: Eight-year clinical outcomes of long-term enzyme replacement therapy for 884 children with Gaucher disease type 1.
evidence.snippet: Anemia, although not severe, was present in >50% of patients at baseline and resolved for all patients after 8 years of treatment.

evidence.reference_title: Gaucher disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001903 | Anemia | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Bone marrow infiltration by Gaucher cells reduces effective hematopoietic reserve and contributes to anemia and thrombocytopenia.
nodes.id: Bone Marrow Infiltration and Hematopoietic Suppression

evidence.reference_title: Eight-year clinical outcomes of long-term enzyme replacement therapy for 884 children with Gaucher disease type 1.
evidence.snippet: Seventeen percent of patients reported a bone crisis before treatment and in the first 2 years of treatment, but no bone crises were reported after 2 years of enzyme replacement therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Gaucher disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002653 | Bone pain | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: This is the case for chitotriosidase and CCL18, which thus constitute quite specific disease biomarkers

evidence.reference_title: Gaucher disease.
evidence.snippet: Monitoring of patients on ERT involves evaluation of growth, blood counts, liver and spleen size and biomarkers such as chitotriosidase which reflect the disease burden.

nodes.description: Associated with cardiac variant with cardiovascular calcification.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: bulbar signs (particularly severe swallowing disorders)

nodes.description: Imiglucerase, velaglucerase alfa, or taliglucerase alfa IV infusions to replace deficient enzyme. Effective for visceral and hematologic manifestations but does not cross the blood-brain barrier, so neurological disease in Types 2 and 3 remains untreated.

evidence.reference_title: Eight-year clinical outcomes of long-term enzyme replacement therapy for 884 children with Gaucher disease type 1.
evidence.snippet: Within 8 years of enzyme replacement therapy, most clinical parameters studied became normal or nearly normal.

evidence.reference_title: Gaucher disease.
evidence.snippet: ERT is of no benefit in type 2 disease.

evidence.reference_title: Quantifying the Erlenmeyer flask deformity.
evidence.snippet: Erlenmeyer flask deformity is a common radiological finding in patients with Gaucher's disease

evidence.reference_title: Gaucher disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0004975 | Erlenmeyer flask deformity of the femurs | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.snippet: Growth retardation (30% of patients) may be the first sign, sometimes associated with cachexia.

evidence.snippet: HP:0012378 | Fatigue | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Gaucher disease: gene frequencies and genotype/phenotype correlations.
evidence.snippet: the presence of a single N370S allele is diagnostic of the type 1 or nonneuronopathic variant

evidence.reference_title: GBA1 / Gaucher disease (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: GBA1 | HGNC:4177 | Gaucher disease | MONDO:0018150 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Biallelic GBA1 pathogenic variants reduce lysosomal glucocerebrosidase activity through catalytic loss, protein misfolding, or impaired delivery to the lysosome. Genotype severity helps shape the clinical path: at least one N370S allele is associated with non-neuronopathic disease, while L444P homozygosity is strongly associated with neuronopathic disease.

evidence.reference_title: Gaucher disease: gene frequencies and genotype/phenotype correlations.
evidence.snippet: the presence of a single N370S allele is diagnostic of the type 1 or nonneuronopathic variant, whereas the L444P/L444P genotype is highly associated with neuronopathic variants in the Caucasian population.

nodes.description: Reduced GCase activity and mutant GCase handling perturb autophagy and alpha-synuclein turnover, creating a GBA1-linked Parkinson disease risk branch that overlaps with but is distinct from classic neuronopathic Gaucher disease.

evidence.reference_title: Neurological effects of glucocerebrosidase gene mutations.
evidence.snippet: In vitro studies have highlighted a bidirectional inverse correlation between GCase activity and alpha synuclein

nodes.description: Lipid-laden macrophages (Gaucher cells) accumulate in spleen, liver, bone marrow, and other organs. These cells have characteristic crinkled tissue paper appearance and cause organ dysfunction through infiltration and inflammatory cytokine release.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: The main cause of the cytopenia, splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, and bone lesions associated with the disease is considered to be the infiltration of the bone marrow, spleen, and liver by Gaucher cells.

nodes.description: Gaucher cell infiltration, vascular compression, inflammatory cytokines, and sphingolipid toxicity disrupt bone remodeling, lower bone mass, and promote infarction or necrotic complications.
nodes.id: Gaucher Skeletal Remodeling and Bone Infarction

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: Psychomotor development is then altered, although some children may still continue to acquire skills.

nodes.description: Deficient beta-glucocerebrosidase (glucosylceramidase) enzyme activity resulting from GBA1 mutations.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: It is caused by a deficiency of the lysosomal enzyme, glucocerebrosidase, which leads to an accumulation of its substrate, glucosylceramide, in macrophages.

nodes.description: Glucocerebroside accumulates primarily in macrophages due to their role in phagocytosis and membrane turnover.
GCase deficiency favors formation and accumulation of glucosylsphingosine (Lyso-Gb1), a soluble bioactive sphingolipid that contributes to neuronopathic toxicity and may also affect bone.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: The main cause of the cytopenia, splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, and bone lesions associated with the disease is considered to be the infiltration of the bone marrow, spleen, and liver by Gaucher cells.

evidence.reference_title: Gaucher disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002240 | Hepatomegaly | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: Some patients present moderate systemic involvement with horizontal ophthalmoplegia as the only neurological symptom

nodes.description: Deficient glucocerebrosidase cannot degrade glucocerebroside (glucosylceramide) in lysosomes, leading to substrate accumulation.
May present as Type 3 with slower neurological progression.

Gaucher disease includes activated monocyte/macrophage states and elevated inflammatory mediators, with chitotriosidase and CCL18 reflecting Gaucher cell burden and cytokines contributing to hematologic and skeletal disease.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: others present more severe forms with varying neurological signs including progressive myoclonus epilepsy (16% of patients)

nodes.description: Most common genotype in Ashkenazi Jewish patients. Always non-neuronopathic (Type 1).
Compound heterozygotes with N370S are Type 1.

Neuronopathic Gaucher disease progresses through neuronal dysfunction and loss in vulnerable CNS regions, producing supranuclear gaze palsy, bulbar dysfunction, pyramidal or extrapyramidal signs, seizures, myoclonus, and developmental impairment.
Microglial activation and neuroinflammation contribute to neurodegeneration in neuronopathic Gaucher disease.

evidence.reference_title: Neurological effects of glucocerebrosidase gene mutations.
evidence.snippet: A common feature appears to be microgliosis and subsequent astrogliosis and neuronal loss, particularly in hippocampal regions

nodes.description: In neuronopathic forms (Types 2 and 3), severe glucocerebrosidase deficiency leads to accumulation of glucosylceramide and glucosylsphingosine in neurons. Glucosylsphingosine is normally absent from healthy brain but accumulates in neuronopathic GD, causing direct neuronal toxicity and death.

evidence.reference_title: Gaucher's disease: clinical features and natural history.
evidence.snippet: Severe deficiency of glucocerebrosidase caused by disabling mutations is additionally associated with neurological manifestations that in part reflect a failure to degrade endogenous neuronal glycosphingolipids

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: the accumulation of glucosylsphingosine may cause neuronal dysfunction and death, leading mainly to GD-related neurological symptoms

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: oculomotor paralysis (or bilateral fixed strabismus) is very suggestive of the disease

evidence.snippet: HP:0000938 | Osteopenia | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Neurological effects of glucocerebrosidase gene mutations.
evidence.snippet: Mutations of the GBA allele are the most significant genetic risk factor for idiopathic PD, found in 5%-20% of idiopathic PD cases depending on ethnicity.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: Patients with type-1 GD-but also carriers of GBA1 mutation-have been found to be predisposed to developing Parkinson's disease

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: Loss of bone mass occurs earlier and is more severe in patients with GD and may cause pathological fractures (of long bones, vertebrae, etc.).

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: Seizures occurring later manifest as myoclonic epilepsy that is resistant to antiepileptic drugs.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of Gaucher Disease Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation and Treatments.
evidence.snippet: hypertonia with pyramidal and possibly extrapyramidal rigidity

evidence.snippet: The main cause of the cytopenia, splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, and bone lesions associated with the disease is considered to be the infiltration of the bone marrow, spleen, and liver by Gaucher cells.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001744 | Splenomegaly | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.snippet: oculomotor paralysis (or bilateral fixed strabismus) is very suggestive of the disease

nodes.description: Miglustat or eliglustat to reduce glucocerebroside synthesis. Eliglustat is first-line oral therapy for Type 1. Miglustat crosses the BBB but has not shown clear neurological benefit.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of oral eliglustat on splenomegaly in patients with Gaucher disease type 1: the ENGAGE randomized clinical trial.
evidence.snippet: treatment with eliglustat compared with placebo for 9 months resulted in significant improvements in spleen volume, hemoglobin level, liver volume, and platelet count.

evidence.reference_title: Eight-year clinical outcomes of long-term enzyme replacement therapy for 884 children with Gaucher disease type 1.
evidence.snippet: More than 50% of patients had platelet counts of <100000 platelets per mm3 at baseline, but >95% had platelet counts above this level after 8 years of treatment.

evidence.reference_title: Gaucher disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001873 | Thrombocytopenia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Gaucher cells accumulate in reticuloendothelial organs, especially spleen and liver, producing splenomegaly and hepatomegaly.
